subject: Chemistry
title: Electron Transitions and Light
description: One-minute skill: Electron Transitions and Light.

hook.text: The specific color a substance absorbs depends on the energy gaps between its electron levels.

buildup.text: Electrons orbit atoms at fixed energy levels. Light can push an electron to a higher level if its energy matches the gap.

discovery.text: Blue light has more energy than red. Substances that absorb red light have smaller energy gaps than those absorbing blue.

twist.text: Transition metals like copper and iron have many energy levels close together, which is why their compounds are so colorful.

climax.text: Neon signs work the opposite way: electricity excites electrons up, then they fall back down and emit specific colors.

punchline.text: Energy gaps set the color. Bigger gap absorbs blue; smaller gap absorbs red.

quiz.question: Why are transition metal compounds often colorful?
options: They have many closely spaced electron energy levels that absorb visible light
